title_en: Zakat and Fasting Responsa in Al-Bayan wal-Tahsil
title_id: Responsa Zakat dan Puasa dalam Al-Bayan wal-Tahsil
content_id:
Responsa zakat dan puasa dalam Al-Bayan wal-Tahsil mengungkapkan bagaimana Imam Malik dan murid-muridnya menerapkan prinsip-prinsip Maliki pada kondisi-kondisi ekonomi dan sosial praktis masyarakat Islam awal, dan bagaimana komentar Ibn Rushd al-Jadd menghubungkan penerapan-penerapan tersebut dengan penalaran fondasi mazhab.

Responsa zakat dalam Al-Bayan wal-Tahsil membahas pertanyaan-pertanyaan yang mencerminkan kompleksitas ekonomi kehidupan perkotaan Islam awal: perlakuan terhadap kemitraan bisnis untuk keperluan zakat, kewajiban zakat tuan tanah yang absen, status barang dalam perjalanan antara pembeli dan penjual di akhir haul (tahun zakat), dan distribusi zakat di komunitas-komunitas di mana beberapa penerima yang memenuhi syarat hadir.

Respons-respons Malik terhadap pertanyaan-pertanyaan ini, sebagaimana ditransmisikan melalui Al-Utbiyyah dan dianalisis oleh Ibn Rushd al-Jadd, mencerminkan keprihatinan khas mazhab Maliki untuk mencegah penghindaran kewajiban-kewajiban zakat melalui dalih-dalih teknis hukum. Malik secara konsisten bersedia melihat melampaui struktur hukum formal dari pengaturan-pengaturan ke substansi ekonominya. Jika sebuah pengaturan dirancang untuk menghindari zakat daripada melayani tujuan komersial yang sah, Malik cenderung memperlakukannya seolah-olah kewajiban zakatnya telah dipicu.

Responsa puasa dalam Al-Bayan wal-Tahsil mencakup pertanyaan-pertanyaan tentang penentuan awal dan akhir Ramadan, sebuah pertanyaan yang secara praktis kritis di komunitas-komunitas yang tersebar di wilayah geografis luas tanpa komunikasi cepat. Bagaimana seharusnya komunitas bertindak ketika para saksi yang dapat dipercaya bersaksi tentang rukyat hilal sementara saksi-saksi yang dapat dipercaya lain menyangkalnya? Bagaimana musafir yang mengamati Ramadan di lokasi berbeda dari tempat ia memulai harus mendamaikan putusan-putusan rukyat yang berbeda? Komentar Ibn Rushd al-Jadd atas pertanyaan-pertanyaan ini menyajikan posisi-posisi mazhab Maliki beserta dasar dalilnya.

Responsa tentang apa yang membatalkan puasa dalam Al-Bayan wal-Tahsil membahas kasus-kasus spesifik yang tidak jelas dicakup oleh aturan-aturan abstrak: orang yang makan mengira masih malam tetapi mendapati hari sudah fajar; orang yang menelan air liur dan bertanya-tanya apakah itu membatalkan puasa; orang sakit yang mengonsumsi obat dan bertanya-tanya tentang statusnya. Respons-respons Malik mengungkapkan prinsip yang konsisten tentang kelenturan untuk kesalahan dan ketidakpastian yang tulus, dikombinasikan dengan kejelasan tentang pelanggaran yang disengaja.

Komentar analitis Ibn Rushd al-Jadd atas kasus-kasus puasa ini mengidentifikasi prinsip mendasar mazhab Maliki: perbuatan-perbuatan yang membatalkan puasa adalah yang melanggar esensi puasa (menahan diri dari makan, minum, dan aktivitas seksual selama siang hari) secara sengaja. Pelanggaran yang tidak disengaja, kesalahan, dan perbuatan-perbuatan karena kebutuhan medis diperlakukan dengan fleksibilitas yang jauh lebih besar. Prinsip ini, yang diartikulasikan dengan jelas dalam komentar Al-Bayan wal-Tahsil, membantu murid menerapkan putusan-putusan puasa mazhab Maliki pada kasus-kasus baru.

Analisis zakat dan puasa dalam Al-Bayan wal-Tahsil menunjukkan nilai literatur responsa sebagai jendela ke dalam bagaimana hukum Islam sebenarnya berfungsi dalam kehidupan komunitas-komunitas Muslim awal, bukan sebagai sistem hukum abstrak melainkan sebagai panduan praktis yang diterapkan oleh ulama-ulama yang sangat peduli terhadap integritas kewajiban-kewajiban keagamaan maupun kesejahteraan orang-orang yang mereka bimbing.
content_en:
The zakah and fasting responsa in Al-Bayan wal-Tahsil reveal how Imam Malik and his students applied Maliki principles to the practical economic and social conditions of early Islamic society, and how Ibn Rushd al-Jadd's commentary connected those applications to the school's foundational reasoning.

The zakah responsa in Al-Bayan wal-Tahsil address questions that reflect the economic complexity of early Islamic urban life: the treatment of business partnerships for zakah purposes, the zakah obligations of absentee landlords, the status of goods in transit between buyers and sellers at the end of the hawl (zakah year), and the distribution of zakah in communities where multiple eligible recipients are present.

Malik's responses to these questions, as transmitted through Al-'Utbiyyah and analyzed by Ibn Rushd al-Jadd, reflect the Maliki school's characteristic concern for preventing the circumvention of zakah obligations through legal technicalities. Malik was consistently willing to look beyond the formal legal structure of arrangements to their economic substance — if an arrangement was designed to avoid zakah rather than to serve a legitimate commercial purpose, Malik tended to treat it as if its zakah obligations were triggered.

The fasting responsa in Al-Bayan wal-Tahsil include questions about the determination of Ramadan's beginning and end — a practically critical question in communities spread over a wide geographic area without rapid communication. How should a community act when reliable witnesses testify to moon-sighting while other reliable witnesses deny it? How should a traveler who observes Ramadan in a different location from where he started reconcile different moon-sighting rulings? Ibn Rushd al-Jadd's commentary on these questions presents the Maliki school's positions with their evidential basis.

The responses on what breaks the fast in Al-Bayan wal-Tahsil address specific cases that the abstract rules do not clearly cover: the person who eats thinking it is still night but discovers it was already dawn; the person who swallows saliva and wonders if that breaks the fast; the sick person who takes medicine and wonders about its status. Malik's responses reveal a consistent principle of leniency for mistakes and genuine uncertainty, combined with clarity about intentional violations.

Ibn Rushd al-Jadd's analytical commentary on these fasting cases identifies the underlying principle of the Maliki school: acts that break the fast are those that violate the fast's essence (abstaining from food, drink, and sexual activity during the day) intentionally. Accidental violations, mistakes, and acts of medical necessity are treated with considerably more flexibility. This principle, clearly articulated in Al-Bayan wal-Tahsil's commentary, helps students apply the Maliki school's fasting rulings to novel cases.

The analysis of zakah and fasting in Al-Bayan wal-Tahsil demonstrates the value of the responsa literature as a window into how Islamic law actually functioned in the lives of early Muslim communities — not as an abstract legal system but as practical guidance applied by scholars who cared deeply about both the integrity of religious obligations and the welfare of the people they guided.
content_ar: The الزكاة and الصيام responsa in Al-Bayan wal-Tahsil reveal how الإمام مالك and his students applied المالكي principles to the practical economic and social conditions of early Islamic المجتمع, and how Ibn Rushd al-Jadd's commentary connected those applications to the school's foundational reasoning. The الزكاة responsa in Al-Bayan wal-Tahsil address questions that reflect the economic complexity of early Islamic urban الحياة: the treatment of business partnerships for الزكاة purposes, the الزكاة obligations of absentee landlords, the status of goods in transit between buyers and sellers at the end of the hawl (الزكاة year), and the distribution of الزكاة in communities where multiple eligible recipients are present. مالك's responses to these questions, as transmitted through Al-'Utbiyyah and analyzed by Ibn Rushd al-Jadd, reflect the المالكي school's characteristic concern for preventing the circumvention of الزكاة obligations through legal technicalities. مالك was consistently willing to look beyond the formal legal structure of arrangements to their economic substance — إذا an arrangement was designed to avoid الزكاة rather than to serve a legitimate commercial purpose, مالك tended to treat it as إذا its الزكاة obligations were triggered. The الصيام responsa in Al-Bayan wal-Tahsil include questions about the determination of Ramadan's beginning and end — a practically البالغ الأهمية question in communities spread over a wide geographic area without rapid communication. How should a الجماعة act عندما reliable witnesses testify to moon-sighting بينما other reliable witnesses deny it? How should a traveler who observes Ramadan in a different location from where he started reconcile different moon-sighting rulings? Ibn Rushd al-Jadd's commentary on these questions presents the المالكي school's positions with their evidential basis. The responses on what breaks the fast in Al-Bayan wal-Tahsil address specific cases that the abstract rules do not clearly cover: the person who eats thinking it is still night but discovers it was already dawn; the person who swallows saliva and wonders إذا that breaks the fast; the sick person who takes medicine and wonders about its status. مالك's responses reveal a consistent principle of leniency for mistakes and genuine uncertainty, combined with clarity about intentional violations. Ibn Rushd al-Jadd's analytical commentary on these الصيام cases identifies the underlying principle of the المالكي school: acts that break the fast are those that violate the fast's essence (abstaining from food, drink, and sexual activity خلال the day) intentionally. Accidental violations, mistakes, and acts of medical necessity are treated with considerably more flexibility. This principle, clearly articulated in Al-Bayan wal-Tahsil's commentary, helps students apply the المالكي school's الصيام rulings to novel cases.
